title: Quantum Simulation of the Real-time Dynamics in the multi-flavor Gross-Neveu Model at the utility scale using Superconducting Quantum Computers
authors: Talal Ahmed Chowdhury, Seokwon Choi, Kyoungchul Kong, Kwangmin Yu
abstract: We present a scalable quantum simulation framework for real-time dynamics of the multi-flavor Gross-Neveu model in 1+1 dimensions. Using superconducting quantum processors at utility scale, we develop a hardware-efficient Trotterization whose per-step circuit depth scales with fermion flavor number rather than total system size, enabling simulations beyond 100 qubits. A central contribution of this work is the Localized Diagonal Operator Approximation (LDOA), which systematically reduces the overhead associated with quartic interactions. We formulate diagonal unitary synthesis as a structured least-squares problem in phase space and obtain analytic solutions via the Moore-Penrose pseudoinverse. This formulation provides a principled and quantitatively controlled approximation: in the small Trotter-step regime, the unitary error is directly linked to the phase reconstruction error and vanishes asymptotically as the Trotter step size decreases. This establishes a clear mathematical foundation for the LDOA while significantly reducing two-qubit gate counts and circuit depth, and is broadly applicable to diagonal quantum operators with long-range structure, making it particularly well suited for quantum hardware with limited qubit connectivity. Using these techniques, we run large-scale simulations on IBM superconducting processors and study real-time observables, including density-density correlators. We benchmark against exact diagonalization and tensor network-based methods, finding strong agreement across system sizes. These results show that combining hardware-aware circuit design with rigorous approximations enables practical near-term simulation of interacting fermionic field theories and provides a scalable pathway toward more complex quantum field theory simulations.
summary: This paper introduces a new approximation method, LDOA, to efficiently simulate the real-time dynamics of the multi-flavor Gross-Neveu model on superconducting quantum computers. By reducing the circuit depth required for complex fermion interactions, the researchers achieved large-scale simulations involving over 100 qubits that match classical results. This work provides a scalable framework for studying interacting field theories on NISQ-era hardware.
main_problem: Simulating the real-time dynamics of the multi-flavor Gross-Neveu model on superconducting quantum processors is hindered by high circuit depth and gate overhead caused by long-range quartic interactions.
main_result: The development of the Localized Diagonal Operator Approximation (LDOA) significantly reduces two-qubit gate counts and circuit depth, enabling large-scale simulations on up to 108 qubits with strong agreement against classical benchmarks.
method: The authors use a hardware-efficient Trotterization scheme combined with LDOA, which reformulates diagonal unitary synthesis as a structured least-squares problem using the Moore-Penrose pseudoinverese, alongside quantum error mitigation techniques like ZNE and Pauli Twirling.
model_or_system: The study focuses on the 1+1 dimensional multi-flavor Gross-Neveu model, a theory of N flavors of Dirac fermions with quartic interactions, implemented on IBM superconducting quantum processors.
key_observables: Density-density correlation functions, second-order Renyi entropy, and purity.
important_parameters: Number of fermion flavors (N), lattice size (L), coupling constant (g), and Trotter time-step (delta t).
main_assumptions: The method assumes a hardware architecture with limited qubit connectivity and utilizes the Kogut-Susskind lattice fermion prescription for discretization.
figures_summary: Circuit diagrams for different flavor numbers (N=2, 3, 4); geometric interpretation of the least-squares projection; comparison of CP and RZZ-based Ansätze; and time-evolution plots of density-density correlations and Renyi entropy comparing hardware, noiseless simulation, and classical benchmarks.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the physical model and the challenge of long-range interactions, presents the LDOA mathematical framework and circuit design, details the experimental implementation on IBM hardware, provides validation against classical methods (ED and MPS-TDVP), and discusses error mitigation and scalability.
why_interesting: It demonstrates a scalable pathway for simulating interacting quantum field theories on near-term hardware by using hardware-aware circuit compression and rigorous mathematical approximations to manage complexity.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 7: Geometric interpretation of a least squares problem. The target vector ⃗b is projected onto the column space Col(A) spanned by a1 and a2 vectors, yielding the closest realizable vector ⃗p = A⃗x for some ⃗x, such that ⃗e = ⃗b −⃗p is orthogonal to the subspace and represents the approximation error due to limited Ansatz expressibility.

Figure 7 illustrates a geometric interpretation of a least squares problem. The target phase vector ⃗b ∈R2n generally lies outside the column space Col(A). As a result, the linear system A⃗x = ⃗b is typically inconsistent, and no exact solution exists within the restricted parameterization. The pseudoinverse resolves this by projecting ⃗b orthogonally onto Col(A), yielding the closest realizable vector ⃗p = A⃗x. The residual vector ⃗e = ⃗b −⃗p is orthogonal to the subspace, as indicated in Fig. 7, and quantifies the irreducible approximation error. The solution ⃗x is given by A+⃗b where the pseudoinverse A+ is (AT A)−1AT . Therefore, it corresponds to the minimum L2 norm parameter vector...

FIG. 8: Comparison of the theoretical landscapes of the CP and RZZ Ansatze as a function of θg. The black solid curve represents the CP Ansatz (Eq. (50)), while the purple dashed curve corresponds to the RZZ Ansatz (Eq. (51)). Each curve shows the L2 norm between the target unitary Utarget(⃗ϕ(θg)) and the Ansatz unitary UAnsatz(⃗θ(⃗x)).

FIG. 14: Comparison of CZ gate depth and CZ gate count between the original circuit and the LDOA-optimized circuit. Fig. 14a shows the CZ gate depth as a function of Trotter steps, while Fig. 14b shows the corresponding total number of CZ gates.

FIG. 15: The time evolution of the density-density correlation in the two-flavor Gross-Neveu model with L = 10 sites with lattice spacing a = 1 and quartic coupling g = 0.5. The left figure shows the results for the LDOA-implemented Trotterization circuit using the CP approximation with the QuSpin exact results (black solid lines), the noiseless Qiskit simulation (blue markers), and the experimental values from ibm boston (red markers) with 2σ uncertainty. The right figure shows the same using the RZZ approximation with the exact results (black solid line), noiseless Qiskit simulation (green markers), and experimental values (purple markers) from ibm boston.
